perturbation.original_text: 2.1. Either party agrees that it shall ensure that it and its associated companies and their respective officers and employees shall keep confidential, both during and after the duration of the agreement, all information which:
perturbation.changed_text: 2.1. Either party agrees that it shall ensure that it and its associated companies and their respective officers and employees shall keep confidential all information which, both during and for a period of one year after the duration of the agreement:
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets or other confidential information, potentially conflicting with South African law on trade secret protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Protection of Information Act
perturbation.law_citation: Section 11(2) of the South African Protection of Personal Information Act 4 of 2013
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no explicit time frame defined for trade secrets in POPIA, Section 11(2) suggests that the responsible party must maintain and protect personal information. Trade secrets often contain sensitive information related to data privacy, limiting the term of confidentiality to one year directly contradicts the reasonable measures expected to comply with POPIA for longer-term protection.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 - Wikipedia Jump to content From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia (Redirected from Protection of Personal Information Act ) Protection of Personal Information Act, 2013 Parliament of South Africa Long title Act to promote the protection of personal information processed by public and private bodies; to introduce certain conditions so as to establish minimum requirements for the processing of personal information; to provide for the establishment of an Information Regulator to exercise certain powers and to perform certain duties and functions in terms of this Act and the Promotion of Access to Information Act, 2000; to provide for the issuing of codes of conduct; to provide for the rights of persons regarding unsolicited electronic communications and automated decision making; to regulate the flow of personal information across the borders of the Republic; and to provide for matters connected therewith. Citation Act No. 4 of 2013 Territorial extent Republic of South Africa Enacted by Parliament of South Africa Assented to 19 November 2013 Commenced 1 July 2020 Keywords data protection right to privacy Status: In force South African privacy legislation This article is about South African privacy legislation. For the food, see Popiah . For the 1976 TV series, see Popi (TV series) . For the Greek singer, see Popi Malliotaki . The Protection of Personal Information Act ( PoPIA or the PoPI Act ) is a piece of legislation which governs the law of data protection and privacy in South Africa . [ 1 ] The act was passed to regulate the right to privacy, as enshrined by section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa , and would work in conjunction with the Promotion of Access to Information Act . The President of South Africa assented to the Act on 19 November 2013. As part of the regulation a new government agency was created, the Information Regulator, [ 2 ] an independent body which is empowered to monitor and enforce compliance of the PoPI Act within the public and privat
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a potential legal contradiction. The original text ensures confidentiality 'both during and after the duration of the agreement,' without specifying a time limit. The changed text limits this confidentiality to 'one year after the duration of the agreement.' The Law Explanation suggests this contradicts POPIA (Protection of Personal Information Act) Section 11(2), because the term of confidentiality may be insufficient to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 2 is an overview of the Protection of Personal Information Act (PoPIA) and states it governs data protection and privacy in South Africa, regulating the right to privacy as per section 14 of the Constitution of South Africa. The one-year limit may not provide the protection the law expects and can therefore contradict the law.

perturbation.original_text: save for employees, consultants or agents of the Business Partner for purposes specifically related to Mandarin’s use of the material.
perturbation.changed_text: save for employees, consultants or agents of the Business Partner for purposes specifically related to Mandarin’s use of the material, provided that such disclosure is made only after obtaining written consent from Mandarin.
perturbation.explanation: Adding a requirement of prior written consent from Mandarin for disclosure of confidential materials to employees, consultants, or agents could conflict with labor laws in South Africa if it unduly restricts the Business Partner's ability to manage their workforce or fulfill their contractual obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 187 of the Labour Relations Act 66 of 1995 (South Africa)
perturbation.law_citation: Section 187 of the Labour Relations Act 66 of 1995
perturbation.law_explanation: The added condition may impede the partner's operational abilities to manage their own resources, which the courts might interpret as constructive dismissal if employment terms are unilaterally changed to the employee's disadvantage. It could also unfairly prevent the Business Partner from doing their own work.
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a new condition requiring Mandarin's written consent before the Business Partner can disclose materials to their own employees, consultants, or agents. This directly impedes the Business Partner's operational abilities and control over their own workforce. The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could potentially conflict with labor laws if it unduly restricts the Business Partner's ability to manage their workforce. The Law Explanation supports this, stating the added condition might impede the partner's ability to manage their resources and could even be interpreted as constructive dismissal or prevent them from doing their work. Even though no legal snippets were provided, the explanations strongly suggest that this change imposes a restriction that did not previously exist which could violate laws regarding employment and management of resources. Therefore, it introduces a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4.  ResRequest connection service4.1 The Business Partner agrees that access to ResRequest information will be used for the sole use of the Business Partner and no auxiliary distribution is permitted, unless agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.  ResRequest connection service4.1 The Business Partner agrees that access to ResRequest information will be used for the sole use of the Business Partner and no auxiliary distribution is permitted, even if agreed upon by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text now prohibits auxiliary distribution, even if both parties agree. This contradicts basic contract law principles that allow parties to modify agreements by mutual consent. Such a clause is likely unenforceable under South African contract law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: South African Contract Law - Freedom of Contract
perturbation.law_citation: Principles of South African Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: South African contract law upholds the principle of freedom of contract, allowing parties to agree and modify terms as they see fit. By disallowing mutual consent for auxiliary distribution, the modified text restricts the parties' contractual freedom, potentially rendering that part of the agreement invalid.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text creates a legal contradiction by prohibiting auxiliary distribution of ResRequest information, 'even if agreed upon by both parties.' This directly contradicts the fundamental principle of freedom of contract, which allows parties to modify agreements by mutual consent. While there are no scraped law snippets to reference, the perturbation explanation and law explanation both state that this violates the freedom of contract in South African law, which would render the new clause unenforceable. The original text allowed for auxiliary distribution if both parties agreed, maintaining contractual freedom, but the change eliminates this possibility, creating a clear contradiction to standard contract law principles.

perturbation.original_text: 6.  Dispute6.1 Any legal dispute arising from this agreement will be subject to the jurisdiction of South African law.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.  Dispute6.1 Any legal dispute arising from this agreement will be subject to binding arbitration in London, UK, under the rules of the London Court of International Arbitration, and governed by English law.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the jurisdiction and governing law from South African law to English law and requiring arbitration in London could be problematic if the contract is primarily performed in South Africa. This could violate mandatory provisions of South African law that cannot be waived by agreement, particularly concerning labor or consumer protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Section 90 of the South African Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008
perturbation.law_citation: Section 90 of the South African Consumer Protection Act 68 of 2008
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 90 of the Consumer Protection Act prohibits contracting out of the provisions of the Act to the detriment of the consumer. Forcing a South African consumer to arbitrate in London under English law could significantly disadvantage them, making the arbitration clause unenforceable.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text introduces a legal contradiction by changing the jurisdiction and governing law from South African law to English law, and mandating arbitration in London. The Law Explanation states that Section 90 of the Consumer Protection Act prohibits contracting out of the provisions of the Act to the detriment of the consumer. Forcing a South African consumer to arbitrate in London under English law could significantly disadvantage them, making the arbitration clause unenforceable. Therefore, a contradiction exists because the modified text potentially violates the Consumer Protection Act by disadvantaging South African consumers. Even though there are no scraped law snippets to reference, the explanation provided describes a clear contradiction.
